Investment Approach

  • Fidelity Contrafund (FCNTX) is an opportunistic, diversified equity strategy with a large-cap growth bias.
  • Philosophically, we believe stock prices follow companies' earnings, and those companies that can deliver durable multiyear earnings growth provide attractive investment opportunities.
  • As a result, our investment approach seeks firms we believe are poised for sustained, above-average earnings growth that is not accurately reflected in the stocks' current valuation.
  • In particular, we emphasize companies with "best of breed" qualities, including those with a strong competitive position, high returns on capital, solid free-cash-flow generation and management teams that are stewards of shareholder capital.
  • We strive to uncover these investment opportunities through in-depth bottom-up, fundamental analysis, working in concert with Fidelity's global research team.

Performance Review

For the first quarter, the fund's Retail Class shares returned -5.39%, lagging the -4.33% result of the benchmark S&P 500® index but outpacing the Morningstar peer average of large growth funds, which returned -8.44%. Stocks entered 2026 with strong momentum but began to fade in late February, as concern about the viability of artificial intelligence-related investments surfaced and conflict in the Middle East took center stage. The Iran war sent a shockwave through oil markets, with the potential to dampen growth and stoke inflation. Amid this environment, we continued our long-standing preference for fast-growing, "best of breed" businesses. Importantly, given our focus, longer-term performance comparisons remain quite favorable.

This backdrop was in stark contrast to the one underpinning the historically fast rally that began in early April 2025, when U.S. stocks were supported by strong corporate fundamentals, a resilient economy, an ongoing boom in spending on AI and, beginning in September, the Federal Reserve's first interest-rate reductions since December 2024.

As 2026 began, investors expected the uptrend to continue, driven by AI-fueled capital expenditures, fiscal support from the massive tax and spending package passed in July 2025, and strong corporate earnings. Instead, the index retreated amid geopolitical uncertainty, persistent inflation and elevated valuations.

In January, the S&P 500® showed signs of fading momentum, largely due to concern about a potential AI bubble, but still gained 1.45%. A lackluster February (-0.76%) produced the first monthly decline since April 2025. Joint U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran began on February 28. The price of crude oil spiked to as high as $120 per barrel following the closing of the Strait of Hormuz, which accounts for roughly 35% of global crude exports. As the conflict spread and other nations and militant groups interceded, the S&P 500® returned -4.98% in March.

In Q1, the growth-oriented consumer discretionary and information technology sectors each returned -9%. Software and services firms (-23%) were particularly weak amid concern about industrywide disruption from AI. Communication services (-7%) was notably hampered by AI-focused market leaders Meta Platforms (META)(-13%) and Alphabet (GOOGL)(-8%) – the parents of Facebook and Google, respectively. The financials sector (-9%) also struggled, as investors worried that major banks could be at risk from their loans to software companies. Health care (-5%) and real estate (+3%) rounded out the laggards.

In contrast, energy (+38%) led by a wide margin, as the price of U.S. crude oil spiked in March. With other commodities also rising, the materials (+10%) and industrials (+5%) sectors outperformed, as did utilities and consumer staples (+8% each).

Security selection in communication services notably detracted from performance versus the S&P 500® in Q1. This mostly reflected a sizable overweight in Meta Platforms (-13%). Despite the firm reporting strong quarterly revenue and earnings, its shares came under pressure amid investor concern about the company's heavy spending on AI and a rotation away from megacap, tech-oriented stocks. Shares declined further in March following a landmark social media addiction case in which a Los Angeles jury found Meta negligent for operating products that harmed children and teens without adequate warnings. We reduced exposure to Meta the past three months, but it remained the largest holding and overweight as of March 31.

In health care, it hurt to underweight Johnson & Johnson (JNJ)(+19%), as the pharmaceutical and medical-products firm topped analysts' expectation for Q4 earnings, thanks to robust drug sales. The stock was further aided by news of a deal with the U.S. administration aimed at lowering drug prices in exchange for tariff relief. We think it's early for a turnaround here, and the company is navigating a loss of exclusivity for a blockbuster autoimmune drug, so we prefer more attractive health care stocks that we think offer faster growth.

We'll also note a negligible stake in integrated oil and gas company Chevron (CVX), established in January. The stock rose 37% in Q1 amid a 44% increase in the price of U.S. crude oil, as measured by the West Texas Intermediate spot price, but the fund largely missed out.

Conversely, positioning among software & services stocks contributed to relative performance, led by an underweight in Microsoft (MSFT)(-23%). Q4 results for the software and cloud-computing giant were not strong enough to mask slowing growth at its Azure cloud business and spending on data centers and other AI infrastructure. Management noted that revenue growth for Azure, while strong in Q4, may be capped by limited availability for AI hardware. We reduced exposure to Microsoft in Q1, making it the No. 2 underweight. Still, it was a sizable holding on March 31.

Our stock picks in the capital goods industry within industrials also stood out, especially a large private investment in Space Exploration Technologies (+32%) and an outsized stake in GE Vernova (+34%), a power-generation business.

Outlook and Positioning

As April begins, the U.S. business cycle is still in expansion after exhibiting a mix of cycle dynamics in Q1. Economic activity was solid for the past three months, despite lingering softness in labor. The backdrop is supported by earnings momentum, fiscal support and wealth-driven high-end consumption.

The powerful AI-led upswing in capex has yet to materially broaden, as labor growth remained tepid. The U.S. dollar rose for the quarter and is richly valued relative to both developed- and emerging-market currencies. After the Fed cut interest rates and ended quantitative tightening in 2025, the central bank had expected two more rate cuts coming into 2026. However, an oil-supply shock – which can drive higher core inflation – led the market to price out those cuts, with expectations shifting to steady policy rates as of March 31.

Inflation remained greater than the Fed's 2% target, as tariffs pushed goods inflation higher while services and housing prices continued to decline modestly. We believe the combination of tariffs, rising energy costs and stable U.S. growth suggests inflation could stay elevated.

Higher capex in Q1 was concentrated in AI-related outlays from large firms in tech and communication services. Job growth was weak during the quarter, though there is little evidence, in our view, that the hiring slowdown can be directly attributed to AI. Unemployment claims and positive real wage growth point to a relatively stable labor market.

U.S. consumers are supported by positive real wage growth and strong household balance sheets. Although lower-income consumers face risk from rising energy costs and weaker balance sheets, consumers in aggregate are less exposed to energy prices than in prior decades marked by oil-supply shocks.

In managing the fund, we follow the investing tenet that a stock's price follows the actual and expected earnings per share of the underlying company over time. So, as the earnings outlook for the fund's holdings changed in Q1, we made only modest adjustments.

A massive capital spending cycle for AI data centers is ongoing. The outcome of this spending – in terms of the return on invested capital it produces – will determine the medium-term fate of the big spenders, including major fund holdings Meta, Amazon.com, Alphabet and Microsoft. We continue to view these as solid, best-in-breed companies, but time will ultimately distinguish enduring business models from weaker ones. Staying ahead of this sorting process is important to us because, combining tech and communication services, the fund is overweight what we consider tech-related "growth plays."

The fund's position in communication services, which includes a sizable commitment to video-streaming service provider Netflix (NFLX), represented 20% of assets and was by far the top sector overweight. Meta is classified as a media name, but we view it as a leading, founder-led tech firm that has embraced AI to improve its business.

Tech represented 26% of assets on March 31, the biggest sector allocation and essentially unchanged from year-end. Noteworthy holdings here are AI-focused chipmaker Nvidia (NVDA), personal-electronics giant Apple, and Amphenol, a maker of electronic connectors.

The fund's exposure to financials was 13%, lower than at year-end and a modest overweight. Insurance-focused conglomerate Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A)(BRK.B) anchors the fund's stake in the sector.

Looking ahead, we will continue to work closely with Fidelity's global team of analysts, which aims to cover virtually every company of significance globally. Therefore, we feel like we have an edge in monitoring every important trend in every business in every market. We see the ability to ask questions of decision makers and adjust the fund's positions, if warranted, as a competitive advantage.

Tracking Error is the divergence between the price behavior of a position or a portfolio and the price behavior of a benchmark, creating an unexpected profit or loss.

data.title: Agios: Novo's Sickle Cell Win Changes The PK Activator Game (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Agios Pharmaceuticals Overview

Agios Pharmaceuticals (AGIO) is a commercial stage biopharma that specializes in PK activation. It actually has one drug that's FDA approved for PK deficiency, branded as Pyrukynd (mitapivat). However, as I've detailed in the past, this is a tiny market. So, I've always believed that Agios' investment thesis hinged on expanding mitapivat into sickle cell disease and thalassemia. While thalassemia looked like a near-blockbuster opportunity, I acknowledged some challenges in sickle cell disease. PK activation undoubtedly had an impact on anemia and hemoglobin, but its long-term benefit in more important endpoints like VOC were still quite unknown. However, given the opportunity in thalassemia and the optionality available in other indications like PK deficiency and sickle cell disease, I had AGIO as a speculative buy. The stock has since underperformed, and in fact, AGIO dropped another 23% yesterday after Novo Nordisk (NVO) announced Phase 3 data in sickle cell disease for its own PK activator. So my purpose of this article is to reevaluate Agios in light of pipeline developments and competition.

Recent Developments

Agios will seek accelerated approval for mitapivat in sickle cell disease. This came after a pre-sNDA meeting in which the FDA “recommended the company submit a proposal for a confirmatory clinical trial that can support a potential accelerated nod for mitapivat in sickle cell disease.”

This reminds me a lot of Pfizer's (PFE) voxelotor, which I used to cover a ton on this platform before Pfizer acquired Global Blood Therapeutics. Voxelotor launched in sickle cell disease after an accelerated approval based merely on hemoglobin response. Pfizer later voluntarily removed voxelotor from the market after it became apparent that the drug increased VOCs and "fatal events."

If Agios secures accelerated approval in sickle cell disease, I doubt that hematologists will be quick to prescribe this until there is confirmatory data. Recall that Phase 3 data were "mixed." Mitapivat boosted hemoglobin, but missed on fatigue and VOC endpoints. Agios is effectively hoping for a voxelotor-like approval path.

Agios did secure approval (“Aqvesme”) for anemia in adults with alpha or beta thalassemia last December. Aqvesme’s label is different than Pyrukynd, likely accounting for the different brand names despite the same drug (mitapivat). It includes a boxed warning for “serious hepatocellular injury” and requires lab tests “at baseline and every 4 weeks for 24 weeks and then as clinically indicated.” Moreover, access to Aqvesme is limited via a restricted program (REMS).

Now, in PK deficiency, the mitapivat dose is titrated from 5 milligrams to a maximum of 50 milligrams twice a day. In thalassemia, patients require higher doses. During the Phase 3 trials in thalassemia, two of 301 patients experienced signs of hepatocellular injury within the first six months (and 3 additional during open-label extension after switching from placebo).

Treatment recommendations I follow (i.e., UpToDate) don't appear "bullish" on mitapivat. For transfusion-dependent thalassemia, main treatments are usually listed as Bristol-Myers Squibb's (BMY) luspatercept and blood transfusions, whereas mitapivat is listed merely as an alternative for some adults with normal liver function.

When I model Aqvesme and compare it with my previous model, the results are sobering.

Inputs:

Outputs:

I have Aqvesme generating no more than $200 million in peak annual sales, worth roughly $358 million in net present value.

Competition

Now, for Novo Nordisk's sickle cell data that was announced Monday. Its own once-daily PK activator, etavopivat, successfully met both co-primary endpoints. It saw reductions in VOC and increases in hemoglobin compared to placebo.

This is very bad news for Agios. Also bear in mind that mitapivat dosing in the Phase 3 portion of RISE UP for sickle cell disease evaluated 100 milligrams twice daily. So, it's possible that the sickle cell disease label, if approved, will also be hit with a boxed warning and REMS.

To counter, Agios has pointed out that:

(...) liver abnormalities observed across the mitapivat and placebo arms were not suggestive of drug-induced hepatocellular injury, unlike what was observed in the mitapivat ENERGIZE and ENERGIZE-T phase 3 trials.

However, this does not guarantee that mitapivat will avoid a boxed warning in sickle cell disease (same dose, same MOA).

Novo Nordisk is also testing its PK activator in thalassemia (Phase 2). Given that PK activation is an established MOA in thalassemia, one would think that etavopivat has good odds here, too.

It's challenging to see how Agios’ PK activator can compete with Novo Nordisk in both sickle cell disease and thalassemia.

And looking at its pipeline, there don't appear to be too many indications beyond the three that I’ve discussed.

Tebapivat

Agios is also developing another PK activator, once-daily tebapivat, but this is in Phase 2 (topline results in sickle cell disease are expected in the second half of this year). Agios describes tebapivat as a “more potent PK activator.” To me, it's a clear acknowledgment that its current PK activator will not be able to effectively compete on the market. It’s understandably a “hedge” as well.

Tebapivat is also in a Phase 2b study in lower-risk MDS (with results expected before July). Notably, Novo Nordisk terminated its trial evaluating its PK activator in MDS due to a low likelihood of the trial showing meaningful patient benefit. So I imagine this has some read-through for Agios. However, tebapivat is not just a PK activator “do-over,” it targets both PKR and PKM2. So, I’m not writing it off just yet.

But even if Agios is able to “crack the case” in MDS, it will still face competition from others, like luspatercept. So, not only does tebapivat need to prove it works in MDS, it needs to show TI rates that are competitive, like in the 30 to 40% range, and/or offer a significantly better safety profile.

AGIO Stock Financial Health

In 2025, Pyrukynd generated $54 million in revenues. This came at an enormous expense. Agios’ SG&A expenses totaled $180.3 million in 2025, while R&D investments totaled $339.5 million.

Agios ended the year with approximately $1.2 billion in cash.

I do not see that the company itself provided a cash runway estimate. I will do my best to provide one myself based on historical data. I go off of "cash used in operations." And in 2025, Agios' cash used in operations was $373 million. If you divide Agios' cash by this figure, the company's cash runway should extend into 2029.

The company guided the U.S. PK deficiency revenue to be in the range of $45 million to $50 million in 2026. Again, I wouldn't expect this indication to support the stock's roughly $1.5 billion market capitalization.

Conclusion/Risks to Thesis

I am really concerned about Agios' immediate prospects in indications like sickle cell disease and thalassemia. After considering competition from the likes of Novo Nordisk, as well as drawbacks like REMS and liver lab monitoring, it's really difficult to see Agios outperforming these markets. And as I've alluded to many times, PK deficiency is a very limited opportunity.

In my mind, Agios has effectively become a clinical-stage biotechnology again, pinning its hopes on its next PK activator in less competitive indications like low-risk MDS.

These developments merit a re-rating ("Hold"). Mitapivat's blockbuster ambitions have bottomed, but Agios' cash hoard and tebapivat-related optionality are not yet supportive of selling near 52-week lows.

There are some things that could change my mind. For instance, if Agios releases really compelling data in low-risk MDS. It's also possible that Novo Nordisk's own PK activator will come with its own issues (there were two drug-related SAEs in Phase 2, including one hepatic enzyme increase), and I might be overestimating the competitive impact. And finally, the company has a couple of other assets in its pipeline that, for purposes of brevity, I did not address within this article, like its PAH stabilizer in phenylketonuria and its siRNA TMPRSS6 in polycythemia vera.

data.title: PennyMac Mortgage Q1 Preview: Yield Holds, But The Cushion Is Gone
data.description:
PennyMac Mortgage Investment Trust (PMT) is an mREIT that invests in mortgage servicing rights (MSRs), credit risk transfer (CRT) assets, and securitized mortgage credit. Its close relation with PennyMac Financial helps it source and structure deals. It earns both spread income and capital gains. Most of the earnings are distributed as dividends — which is why the yield is high. However, returns are driven by prepayment behavior, margins at origination, and securitization returns.

PMT is a high-yield vehicle trading at a discount. It is supported by a business driven by three factors — prepayments, margins and portfolio returns. PMT’s yield is ~13% (FWD') and it trades at ~0.8x book: these are already high numbers so upside is not the concern here. The discussion is more about whether the business can continue to support its ~$0.40 quarterly distribution.

This makes the company’s earnings release (scheduled for May 5, 2026) important. The consensus view is that the company will be able to support its dividend. We saw this in Q4, where the $0.48 EPS easily supported the dividend. Management’s latest guidance shows that the gap between EPS and dividend will narrow significantly.

Here, management said the following:

Turning to Slide 10, you can see the average quarterly run rate return potential expected from PMT's investment strategies over the next 4 quarters. PMT's current run rate reflects a quarterly average of $0.40 per share, down slightly from $0.42 per share in the prior quarter. As I noted earlier, we expect increased investments in accretive non-agency subordinate and senior bonds, primarily through organic securitization activity. Our expected returns from the interest rate-sensitive strategies remains unchanged from the prior quarter as lower return potential from MSRs due to higher prepayment expectations was offset by a decrease in projected hedge costs. In correspondent production, margins have declined, and our expectations for returns from the strategy are down from the prior quarter.

So this is something investors should be keenly watching. If the gap doesn’t narrow, the dividend remains covered. Valuation also seems fine. If the gap narrows as guided, or worse, the stock becomes risky — because there’s no margin for error.

Where We Stand Today

The next earnings release is critical because PMT may look stable on the surface, but there’s some tension underneath. It looks stable because the most recent quarter still supports the dividend. However, that stability collapses when we look at forward metrics.

Management itself has guided to around $0.40 implied quarterly run rate, down from earlier guidance of $0.42. This may seem small, but since the dividend here is also $0.40, this is cutting it too short. There is no margin of safety any more — this is just breakeven coverage.

The same tightening can be seen in the business itself. Higher prepayment has caused pretax income to fall from $32M in Q3 to $28M in Q4 (income excluding market-driven value changes fell from $36M to $21M). Those are figures for the interest rate–sensitive strategies. But even in the production segment also, there’s been a small $1M loss at the pretax level. The number may be small, but the direction is not reassuring. It shows that the company is now increasingly dependent on higher-return, riskier credit assets as well as active portfolio rotation to maintain overall returns.

They haven’t changed the dividend, however, which still stands at $1.60 annually. Consensus does not expect any adjustment in 2026. Payout ratio, at ~163%, is elevated on a GAAP basis, which shows the difference between accounting earnings and distributable income.

This may not be a company under stress, but it is close to its limits. If execution holds and the business stays stable, this may work — and Q1 earnings will give us some indication of whether it will work.

What Changed Since Last Quarter

Very little has really changed if we look from the headline level. PMT is still profitable, the dividend is still covered, and book value is stable. What concerns me is the direction.

Net income declined from $48M in Q3 to $42M in Q4, with EPS moving from $0.55 to $0.48, still above the $0.40 dividend. Also, as we noted in the earlier section, there were losses in both the interest rate–sensitive strategies as well as the production segment.

To offset this loss, PMT has increasingly shifted towards securitization-driven credit strategies. In Q4 alone, they did 8 securitizations totaling $2.8B in UPB. The total in 2025 came to 19 transactions and $6.7B in UPB, with retained investments rising to $528M.

This was not additive. It came from selling active portfolio positions and buying new ones. PMT sold $195M of GSE CRT investments which saw compressed forward returns. The new allocations targeted returns in the low- to mid-teens. PMT also added $876M of agency floating-rate MBS, which is geared towards profiting from the current rate environment.

This is an important shift because PMT is not relying on legacy assets any more to generate returns. What is being done instead is actively recycling capital from lower-yielding positions into hopefully higher-return ones. These are more execution-dependent strategies. While this may increase returns — potentially — it also depends on continuous deal flow.

What I Am Watching This Quarter

This quarter’s earnings are important, but PMT is too multi-layered for only headline numbers to matter. What truly matters if the earnings level will hold at $0.40 or better that number, especially when broken into its underlying drivers.

There are five things that matter here.

  1. If reported EPS moves back toward even ~$0.42, PMT gets some much-needed cushion. The dividend also looks safer. If it stays at $0.40, PMT stays stable but tight. If it drops, even a little, the discussion changes — because there is no longer any margin between earnings and payout.

  2. In Q4 itself, the company noted that prepayments were a headwind. This was especially so for MSR-related income. While absolute numbers matter, the direction matters more here. If prepayment speed stabilizes, there will be less pressure on these interest rate–sensitive strategies. Otherwise, these strategies will not work well, and the rest of the portfolio will have to take up the slack.

  3. The production segment has turned into a weak spot. While small, it posted a pretax loss last quarter, from competitive pressure and spread compression. Neither issue is one-off. If margins remain negative, it makes you wonder if PMT’s earnings base is structurally weakening.

  4. This has turned into the core story. Management is targeting around 30 securitizations in 2026. Expected returns are in the low- to mid-teens. Whether that works — not only in terms of volume but also on returns — is something to be watched. If these deals are not able to offset weakness elsewhere, the entire earnings structure becomes fragile.

  5. Last quarter, book value had remained stable. It must continue to do so this quarter as well. A stable book value shows risk control and discipline. When book value declines alongside earnings coverage, that indicates PMT is sacrificing long-term value for current dividend.

Putting all these ideas together, this quarter is not about performance, but about efficiency. We want to see numbers that demonstrate PMT is in control of the narrative.

Valuation: Cheap, But Not Mispriced

PMT’s current price is $12.09. The current dividend is $1.60 annually and ~13% is the yield. The stock is priced for this yield level. However, the situation is tight. Management’s implied run rate is exactly equal to this dividend on a per quarter basis. So the current payout does support the current price, but barely.

Now look at the downside. Lets say earnings goes to ~$0.35 per quarter ($1.40 annually). Then, to maintain the same ~13% yield, the stock would need to be priced closer to ~$10.77. That is roughly ~10% downside.

Now suppose earnings recovers towards ~$0.42 per quarter (~$1.68 annually). Then the stock should move to ~$12.9 — a modest 5% upside. If we want a stronger 20% upside, the run-rate should have to move to ~$0.48 — or the earlier peak levels.

So PMT may appear cheap at ~0.8x book and ~7.4x forward earnings, but it is not mispriced.

Conclusion

At current levels, I rate PMT a hold for its yield. The ~13% distribution is supported as long as earnings hold around the ~$0.40 quarterly run rate. There is no need for multiple expansion if that holds steady.

However, I don’t call it a buy because there is little margin of error. Unless Q1 earnings show something huge — EPS back to Q3 levels, for example — the risk is too much. This is true especially given how far the stock fell in February, after the slight EPS decline we saw in Q4.

data.title: Tesla: The SpaceX IPO Won't Save This Stock From Gravity
data.description:
Introduction

Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) has shed over 18.6% since my last analysis, which attributed the company a sell rating despite an oil shock that could have shifted the narrative toward a bullish trend for electric vehicles. I want to dive in the recent developments, such as the SpaceX (SPACE) IPO and the incoming quarter, and how this could re-rate Tesla’s stock.

The SpaceX IPO

The SpaceX IPO will target a valuation of $1.75T and a capital raise of up to $75B, which will be simply enormous as it will directly create, if the valuation holds, the sixth most valuable public company on earth. This creates a sort of unique issue for Tesla, as SpaceX will create a direct competitor for investor capital that wants to gain exposure to Elon Musk. See, most investors believe that this is bullish for Tesla as it removes the overhang of Musk selling TSLA shares to fund SpaceX capex and validates the broader Musk ecosystem, but I believe that this is backward.

As I mentioned, Tesla has been the only publicly tradable vehicle for broad Musk exposure, as investors who wanted a piece of Starlink or rockets had no choice but to buy the stock. All the high volatility linked to Musk will now be split between two public vehicles and the one with the high revenue growth will certainly attract more dollars.

SpaceX will also reportedly allocate around 30% of IPO shares to retail, which is 3 times the typical Wall Street standard, meaning that the demand will be enormous and with the $75B in capital raised, the cash will have to come from somewhere and I suspect that it will come from investors trimming the other Musk-linked position. Passive funds and ETFS with caps on single-founder or thematic such as Musk exposure will also be forced to sell TSLA to buy the SpaceX IPO to maintain balanced allocations.

There has also been growing speculation about a potential merger of SpaceX and Tesla, and though no official proposal has been made, there are several dynamics suggesting the companies are already trending toward a convergence. For example, the Terafab JV is the first concrete step toward a full merger as the facility would consolidate chip manufacturing for both orbital and terrestrial AI, reducing the logistical and financial barriers between the companies. I don’t believe that this will solve the problems, as there could be a risk considering the super-sized entity that could become a failed conglomerate, akin to General Electric, where the complexity and size of the business spanning different sectors might actually lead to inefficiency. But this is all speculation.

What to look for in the incoming earnings

I believe that the most single important line of item will be the automotive gross margins as they have recovered from 15.4% in Q1 to 17.9% in Q4 2025, but this was largely driven by cost cuts and regulatory credit sales, not actual volume leverage. If margins slip back below 17% on weaker Q1 volumes and pricing pressure, it will confirm that the auto business cannot self-fund the $20B+ CapEx programme. Though if the figure holds over 18% despite the delivery miss, that would signal pricing discipline and cost efficiency that would stabilise the bear case.

I’ll also look for the 50,000 unit gap between production and deliveries is also a flashing warning sign and if this spread persists or widens, it will mean that Tesla is building vehicles it cannot sell at current prices, a classic inventory overhang that historically precedes another round of price cuts and margin compression. Tesla will need to close the gap through demand recovery and not production curtailment.

The CapEx guidance for 2026 is also high, but Tesla has explicitly excluded Terafab from that guidance. Musk’s team has apparently contacted multiple suppliers, suggesting that the project is moving beyond concept. It will be intriguing to see if the quarterly call provides any Terafab phasing or cost guidance, it will further accelerate the capital intensity narrative, with potentially a $10B annual spend increase on top of the existing CapEx, pushing the total investment well beyond what the auto business can fund organically.

Valuation

My biggest bearish take on Tesla has always been its ludicrous valuation, and this trend has continued with its P/E Non-GAAP reaching nearly 240, an insane level when considering that competitor BYD (BYDDF) has a P/E of 11.8 while even Nvidia (NVDA) is “only” at 42.19. On an FCF basis, even if it is positive, the company is only yielding 0.4% at current prices.

I also want to do an SOTP analysis on the firm, as Tesla is now having its hands in multiple ventures so I believe that it is important to grasp the value behind them. The first part would be the existing businesses, mainly the auto segment, valued at 0.8x the estimated full-year revenue for 2026, and I believe that this is generous given that BYD is trading at around 0.5x while legacy carmakers are more toward 0.3x. I rate Energy storage at 4x revenue, considering that clean energy peers traditionally range between 3 to 5x, while services are at 2x and regulatory credits at 3x, but declining, considering the current landscape. This gives these segments a $42 per share price.

On the moonshot projects that Tesla is currently undertaking, meaning Robotaxi, FSD, Optimus, Semi and AI/Terafab, it is relatively hard to gauge what value these projects have and how they will contribute in the future. Based on their probable TAM and assumed market share, this gives the segment a $72 per share. In all, with the $460B equity value, this would only give Tesla a $123 per share price tag.

Risks to my bearish stance

Mass production of the Optimus humanoid robot is scheduled to begin at the Fremont factory this summer with an ultimate target of 1MM units per year. If factory trials at Giga Texas demonstrate immediate labor-cost parity, it would certainly trigger a re-rating of the robotics segment, pushing the stock price upward.

Tesla also shifted to an exclusive full self-drive ("FSD") subscription model in February, and with potential regulatory approvals in China and Europe, it could drive higher-than-modeled software margins. If the current 12% take rate surprises to the upside, the resulting high-margin revenue could fund Tesla’s R&D cycle without further stressing the balance sheet.

Conclusion

With the unprecedented capital reallocation event being the Space IPO, as well as the continuous ludicrous valuation level, I do not believe that rational investors should invest in Tesla ahead of its quarterly earnings. It is priced as if the Robotaxi and Optimus programs are already generating high-margin revenue, which is simply not the case. With investors likely given the choice between a cash-flow positive, monopoly-positioned SpaceX and a cash-burning, competition-ridden Tesla, I believe that the reallocation of capital will be swift. I keep my Sell rating on Tesla.

data.title: AST SpaceMobile Got Delivered To The Wrong Address
data.description:
AST SpaceMobile—Introduction and Investment Thesis

AST SpaceMobile (ASTS) has confirmed the total loss of its BlueBird 7 satellite after Blue Origin’s New Glenn rocket placed the spacecraft into a fatally low orbit during the mission from Cape Canaveral, sending shares a little over 5% down yesterday on 04/20.

This would have been the ASTS’s eighth deployed satellite in the constellation, as part of the company’s FY26 target to launch 45-60 satellites and at least double their revenue on a year-over-year basis to $150-$200 million.

I last wrote about ASTS in January, when I initiated my “Hold” rating on the stock based on my reservation against possible delays or execution errors with satellite launches that could derail the company from delivering against their forward targets and contract valuation multiples.

Since then, the stock is down 20%, underperforming the S&P 500, as can be seen below.

With the loss of the BlueBird 7 satellite, ASTS’s FY26 target is indeed at risk, as the company now has to put at least 40 satellites in a span of eight months, leaving little room for execution error from here onwards.

While the financial risk associated with the satellite’s cost is mitigated through its insurance policy, it nonetheless delays the pace of progress, especially as the valuation of the company is built around the scaling of its Commercial revenue along with momentum in its Government segment.

As of Q4 FY25, the company had secured $1.2 billion in revenue commitment from commercial contract partners that include MNOs (Mobile Network Operators) across continents and had projected FY26 to be an “inflection year” for commercial service as they enter commercial service with their initial MNO partners while also continuing to generate revenue from the commercial gateway and government strategies.

While no MNO partners have backed off following the news of the failed launch, setbacks such as this will push the timeline of commercial revenue recognition further into 2027.

The good news so far is that the company has a strong balance sheet, and management has reiterated that BlueBird 8 through BlueBird 10 remain on track with delivery expected within approximately 30 days.

However, with the elevated execution risk, I will remain on the sidelines, at least until the company reports its Q1 earnings on 05/12, where we may get clarity on updated guidance on revenue and the number of satellites projected to launch in FY26, along with how management is thinking on navigating the disruption from Blue Origin’s New Glenn rockets that may ground subsequent missions for longer than expected, forcing the company to leverage its alternative launch providers.

The Loss Of BlueBird 7 Puts ASTS’s FY26 Satellite Launch Target At Risk

ASTS has confirmed the total loss of its BlueBird 7 satellite after Blue Origin’s New Glenn rocket placed the spacecraft into a fatally low orbit during the mission from Cape Canaveral.

As I laid out in my previous post, ASTS is building the world’s first space-based cellular broadband network that is designed to connect directly to standard, unmodified smartphones, where every BlueBird satellite is, in effect, a cellular tower in low Earth orbit, covering territory that no ground-based network reaches.

After the successful launch of its BlueBird 6, which management described as "the largest ever commercial communication array deploying load at orbit" in their Q4 FY25 earnings call, they had laid out expectations for their BlueBird 7 (identical to BlueBird 6) to launch in March. This would then be followed by one to two stacked launches (consisting of three, four, six, and eight satellites per launch) per month on average across the full year, which would allow them to reach their target of 45 to 60 total satellite launches during FY26 as they make progress towards commercial service activation in the second half.

However, with the loss of BlueBird 7, the commercial target of 45-60 satellite launches by FY26 comes under question. Note that BlueBird 7 was intended to become the eighth satellite in the constellation, and its loss now means the company must now recover that slot through faster production and an additional successful launch.

The good news is that management hasn’t walked back on their full-year deployment target. BlueBirds 8 through 10 are still on target to be shipped in around 30 days, while the company is already in production through BlueBird 32. Also, the satellite’s cost is covered by its insurance policy, which removes the most immediate balance sheet impact.

However, Blue Origin’s anomaly investigation process could negatively impact ASTS’s constellation delivery timeline, especially as the company has a multi-launch agreement with Blue Origin for subsequent BlueBird deliveries. While ASTS has alternative launch relationships with SpaceX's Falcon 9 and India’s ISRO LVM3, there is still considerable uncertainty as to whether the company can indeed deliver on its FY26 target.

As Shay Boloor from Futurum Equities pointed out, “In a story like AST, time matters almost as much as capital because the whole valuation is built around deployment cadence, constellation progress, commercial readiness, and proving that the company can keep stacking execution milestones.”

In this case, with only 6 satellites currently in orbit, ASTS must now successfully launch and deploy 40 more spacecraft in the remaining eight months of the year, leaving virtually no room for execution error for the remainder of the year, in my opinion.

Let’s Address The Big Picture

For the full year FY26, ASTS expected to double their revenue versus FY25, with guidance set between $150 million and $200 million, in addition to the planned 45-60 satellite launches.

This comes on the back of their FY25 revenue, where the company generated $70 million in revenue on the upper end of their guidance, driven primarily by commercial gateway deliveries and milestones completed from government contracts.

While Government revenue is an early reliable contributor to ASTS’s revenue, it is the build-up of the momentum in their Commercial segment that has investors excited. In H2 2025, the company delivered 15 commercial gateways to MNO partners across five continents in preparation for SpaceMobile commercial service as they expanded their partner ecosystem with 50 global MNOs with nearly 3 billion subscribers and major customer deals that included Verizon, stc Group, AT&T, and Vodafone. As of the previous earnings call, ASTS had secured over $1 billion in total contracted revenue commitment from their commercial partners.

Looking forward into 2026, the management explicitly stated that they would be scaling their commercial operations from initial commercial activation to the start of commercial service with MNOs across key markets like the United States, Europe, Japan, Saudi Arabia, and others, with more expected to join, along with the U.S. government.

What is also important to note is that their full-year revenue guidance does not account for the commercial service revenue that they expect to generate from their initial MNO partners later in the year, which derisks their guidance to a certain extent.

Looking ahead to 2027, management expects strong revenue growth from both Commercial and Government service revenues, as their cellular broadband service becomes available in “some of the best markets worldwide to hundreds of millions of subscribers via a low-friction service offering provided when the subscriber needs it most.”

At the same time, they also expect government revenues to multiply in 2027, with the opportunity to approach $1 billion in annual revenue comprising both long-term contracts and recurring revenue across both Commercial and Government segments.

While management is optimistic about their long-term revenue upside driven by greater subscriber uptake and market extension across segments in the coming years, they did make it a point to reiterate that it would be contingent on the successful launch and deployment of Block 2 BlueBird satellites related to U.S. government applications and service revenues in connection with the activation of their commercial service provided by their existing and planned satellite launches.

In other words, the loss of the BlueBird 7 satellite could indeed derail the activation and scaling timeline of their Commercial service to a certain extent, while sustained launch delays (and therefore a high chance of missing the 45-60 satellite launch target in 2026) in the future could significantly push commercial revenue recognition further into 2027.

The good news is that none of its MNO partners have pulled out following the launch failure, meaning its $1.2 billion contracted backlog is still unchanged, as partners view this incident to be a third-party launch failure, rather than a flaw in ASTS’s satellite technology.

Therefore, while the underlying fundamental thesis in the company is not broken per se, the launch-related setback does delay the pace of the company’s progress in scaling its revenue goals ahead.

ASTS Stock—Assessing Investment Thesis and Conclusions

Looking at consensus estimates, analysts are projecting ASTS to grow their revenues by 155% YoY in FY26 to $181 million. This should be followed by 329% YoY in FY27 to $772 million as the full impact of their Commercial service scaling takes place. However, what is important to note is that these revenue projections are heavily dependent on the successful launches and deployment of their BlueBird satellites, which I previously discussed. In other words, these revenue projections are not set in stone and may be subject to downward revisions in the event of execution error, like the one we witnessed with the loss of BlueBird 7 satellite.

When it comes to profitability, ASTS continues to operate at a loss and is expected to remain that way at least until FY28, as can be seen below.

The good news is that inclusive of cash raised in February via the convertible notes offering, their total position stands at $3.9 billion as of December 31, which management noted is enough to fund the manufacturing and launching of a constellation of over 100 satellites to provide worldwide space mobile service, with no further plans for additional convertible debt.

However, when looking at Wall Street price targets, we can see the average price target sits just 6.7% higher than current levels, while the potential upside and downside to the “high” and “low” price targets stand at 44% and 49%, respectively, which does not offer an attractive risk reward at current levels.

The way I see it, a drawdown to the $66-$72 range would be a “better” entry point, which coincides with the 50Wk moving average and the 200DMA, offering a potential upside of roughly 24% to Wall Street’s average price target.

Bottom line, I will remain on the sidelines with ASTS, especially as I anticipate further volatility into its Q1 earnings next month on 05.12, where we might get clarity from management’s updated guidance on the projected number of satellite launches in FY26, their revised constellation timeline that accounts for BlueBird 7 loss, along with potential revisions to their revenue target. What would also be important to monitor is how management is thinking about navigating a potentially extended period of Blue Origin’s New Glenn disruption by leveraging their alternative launch providers to de-risk their constellation timeline.

At this point, if management can still confidently present a roadmap towards launching a total of 45 satellites (an additional 40 over the next eight months) that doesn’t solely depend on New Glenn, I believe it will add some credibility. But, given the elevated execution risk at this point, with fairly unattractive risk-reward, I will reiterate my “Hold” rating at current levels.

data.title: Lotus Bakeries: Still Too Expensive Despite Double-Digit Growth Profile
data.description:
Introduction

Belgium-based Lotus Bakeries (LOTBY) is the owner and producer of the rapidly becoming iconic Biscoff cookie (and also owns the Nakd and Trek granola and fruit bar brands). The company has been somewhat a "victim" of its own success, as its production capacity has been insufficient to keep up with demand growth. This is now being remedied with a new facility that has come online in the US, with a new plant in Thailand expected to start production later this quarter.

Unfortunately this doesn't mean the stock is cheap right now, and as it has been a while since I last looked at it, I wanted to check if I need to revise my position.

Lotus Bakeries has its main listing in Belgium, where it is listed with LOTB as its ticker symbol on Euronext Brussels. The current market cap is approximately 8.8B EUR. I will use the euro as the base currency throughout this article.

Strong cash flows, but the stock remains expensive

Before discussing the company's potential growth trajectory, I wanted to have another look at 2025, considering those results will be the stepping stone that will determine the potential revenue and cash flow going forward.

As the income statement below shows, Lotus continues to grow its revenue with a 10% increase in 2025. Meanwhile, the operating expenses increased at a slower pace, which resulted in an underlying EBIT of 232M EUR. This represents an increase of in excess of 12% compared to 2024. Meanwhile, the total amount of net finance expenses remained pretty stable, which means the pre-tax income jumped by 12.5%, while the net income increased from just under €153 million to in excess of €172 million.

This resulted in an EPS of just over 212 EUR per share, a very noticeable increase from the just under 188 EUR per share generated in 2024.

In my previous articles I of course focused on the company's cash flow statement as well, considering the required sustaining capex to operate a cookies plant is pretty low. While this doesn't immediately shine through in the cash flow statement, considering Lotus has been investing in growing its manufacturing capacity, it remains obvious the income and cash flows are more than sufficient to cover the growth initiatives.

As you can see below, the total amount of operating cash flow was approximately €206 million, but we should deduct approximately 10 million in taxes owed but not paid (based on the FY 2025 income statement) and add back the 22 million in working capital changes.

On the other hand, I should also deduct the €13 million in lease payments and interest payments. This results in an underlying operating cash flow of approximately 205M EUR.

The total capex was approximately €113 million, of which the vast majority obviously was spent on the new plants. And despite this heavy spending - which will come down from the second semester of this year on once the plant in Thailand has been completed – Lotus was clearly able to cover all of its investments without having to borrow any additional money. The total gross debt did increase somewhat, but the excess cash was invested in bonds or added as cash on the balance sheet to ensure superior access to liquidity in the near term.

The total amount of net debt came in just below €200 million, which is less than 0.8 times the underlying EBITDA.

Where to go from here?

Based on the financial results from the past few years, Lotus bakeries is way too richly valued. While the company continues to grow pretty fast, it's simply not a good idea to pay about 50 times earnings for what essentially is a producer of cookies.

But it would of course be wrong to invest based on a look in the rearview mirror, and as the new capacity in Thailand is slated to come online later this year, Lotus Bakeries should be judged based on its potential for the next few years.

I think it's quite realistic to expect a 25% EBITDA increase between 2025 and 2027 (the latter will be the first year with the Thai operations contributing for an entire financial year), followed by an additional high single-digit EBITDA increase in 2028.

Thanks to the very robust balance sheet and the low net interest expenses, I anticipate that about 90% of the incremental EBITDA will flow through to the pre-tax income (with the remainder related to the increased depreciation expenses). So a 100M EUR EBITDA increase between 2025 and 2028 should likely result in a 80-85M EUR boost to the pre-tax income and provide a 60M EUR boost to the bottom line result.

This means that for the financial year 2028, I now expect a net income of approximately 230M EUR. Divided over 813,000 shares outstanding (I’m anticipating a 1% net share count increase versus the average in 2025), this would result in an EPS of 280-285 EUR per share. This includes the impact of the new operations in Thailand as well as assuming increased volumes from joint ventures with other food related producers, like, for instance, the joint venture with Mondelez (MDLZ).

Investment thesis

While I was hopeful that the share price decrease we saw in the first few months of this year would continue and make Lotus Bakeries drop down to a level that would be more appropriate (20x earnings would be a good start), that's unfortunately not the case. Even if I'm applying a 40% EBITDA increase between now and FY 2028, this stock is still trading at approximately 35 times the forward earnings for 2028.

So while I fully acknowledge the company is very well run and we can expect the revenue and EBITDA to continue to grow at a high single-digit percentage after the new Thai plant starts up, there isn't a single metric making the company attractive to me from an investment point of view. That's why I'm currently a "hold" on the stock, and I'm being quite generous due to its track record. I currently have no position in Lotus Bakeries and no intention to initiate a long position anytime soon.
